speaker: interviewer
text: Today's November 16, 1995. The survivor is Leon Leyson, L-E-Y-S-O-N. Interviewer is Stella Eliezrie. We are in Fullerton, California, United States of America. And the language will be English. Today is November 16, 1995. We are in Fullerton, California, United States of America. My name is Stella-- S-T-E-L-L-A-- Eliezrie-- E-L-I-E-Z-R-I-E-- and I have the honor of interviewing Leon-- L-E-O-N-- Leyson-- L-E-Y-S-O-N. And the language will be in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today is November 16, 1995. We're in Fullerton, California, United States of America, and I have the honor of interviewing Leon Leyson. My name is Stella Eliezrie. Good afternoon, Leon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon, Stella.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me what was your name-- what was the name that you were given when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was [? Labol ?] Leyson. Labol. My Hebrew was [? Arya. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old are you today?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a small town in eastern Poland called Narewka.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you now about your parents. Could you please tell me about your father? What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Moshe. And my mother's name was Anna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a machinist. He was tool and dye maker. He started in Narewka, and then he eventually transferred with a company to a place called Krakow, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was that from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, from Narewka, Krakow was approximately 350 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did he commute?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not very often. He-- he actually as a young man left Narewka to seek his fortune in Krakow, because in a small town like Narewka, there was not too many opportunities for a young man at his age. He was in his 30s, and so when the company moved, he was asked to relocate. And so he left Narewka and his family and went to establish himself in the big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't he take his family with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- would have been very difficult to-- for a young man in his 30s just getting started to take his family to a big city. It was-- besides, in those days, people didn't move very much, and my mother thought that it would be easier for her with five children to stay in Narewka, where she had her-- her parents, my grandparents, and my father's parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we children were looked after by both grandparents, and so it was much easier that way. It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was thought that it was-- wasn't going to be very long before he could bring his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of father was he to his children, to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was a-- an easygoing kind of father. He-- he encouraged us, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was not of an aggressive kind of person. Patient.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he provide well for his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did. Yes, he was very proud of that, because the family originally was-- his own family was not a rich family, and each-- each member of the family had to work hard to-- to provide for themselves and for their own families. So he was a proud provider, and he provided very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you about your father's religious observance. Was he a traditional Jew? Was he-- did he take you to synagogue as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did. You know, in Poland in the '30s, there weren't many denominations of-- of the Jewish religion. There weren't-- there was Orthodox, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was not exactly Orthodox. But his family was. So we used to go to a temple in-- in Krakow once we moved to Krakow, and he used to take us to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in Narewka? Did he take you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Narewka there was one synagogue, and everyone was Jewish. There was no such thing as something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me know a little about your mother. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Anna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name is Golner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she from?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was from not Narewka. Her family-- not everyone in her family was from Narewka, but it was-- people didn't travel very far, so you were either from Narewka or the surrounding area, about a circle of maybe 40, 50 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother was responsible, then, for taking care of the children. How many of you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were five children in our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she the disciplinarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was. Yeah, that's right. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she do a good job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, I think she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she strict?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we turned out OK. Yes, she was strict, but she was a very kind woman. But she had four boys and-- and a girl, and some of the boys needed some discipline. So that's what she did. But we were looked after by the grandparents as well, so it was really an extended family and warm. And either my father's side they sort of lived on either side of the street from us. And so when we moved-- when we went to my father's parents, it was a warm place to be. And in my recollection, when I was young, living in Narewka was quite comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your city. How many Jewish people lived in Narewka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Narewka was the total population was approximately 2,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of those were Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: About half. About half the population in Narewka was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this considered a town or city?

speaker: interviewee
text: A town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you just describe to us what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's-- it's a little difficult. All the houses, of course, were wooden, and they all had gardens in the front and in the back. We grew vegetables. For example, in our home, we grew vegetables in the back of the house and flowers in the front. And it was quite pretty, actually. It was very quiet. Simple, very simple town. Water was drawn from a well. No inside plumbing. And transportation was either walking or by horse and wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have opportunities as a child to go to a larger city to get a sense of what a big city might feel like?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't see a big city until our family finally joined my father in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1938. I was just going to turn eight-- nine years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you actually when your father moved to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was probably about two years old, because he moved when he was a young man, in approximately 1932 or something like that. In 1932, he was 34 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get to know him very well, then, between the age of two and eight? How often were you actually-- were you able to see him actually?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he-- he used to come when he had his vacations. During vacation time, he would come in and used to be about every six months or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long would he stay for?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple of weeks. It was a big occasion when my father came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you happy to see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, and not only that, but it was sort of a big occasion for the whole town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a-- their own native son from a small town comes to visit the little town, people who don't travel very much. It was an occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember a gift that he might have brought for you? Did he bring gifts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. He did. He used to bring gifts, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would he bring you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- well, practical things.

speaker: interviewer
text: For instance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he would send us shoes and school bags. They were more like briefcases in those days. And I recall when he brought us these briefcases, and they were various sizes. And I only saw two of them. And so I thought, well, the one was for my older brothers, and I wasn't going to get one. But there were some inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. So you got one also.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me before we continue. What were the names of your brothers and sister? My oldest brother was Hershel. And then next to him was [? Salig. ?] His name was [INAUDIBLE]. We called him [? Salig ?] for short. And then my sister, who's now known as Aviva. What was she called as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pescha. And then my brother David and me. I was the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your education as a child. Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all went to the same school. There was only one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] public school, and it actually went to the sixth grade and then after that one had to travel to another-- a larger city like [INAUDIBLE] start to continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a good student?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was not such a good student. My-- my brothers and sisters were better. But I was-- I had an advantage because whatever they learned I learned as well. So when I went-- when it was my time to go to school, I had already learned some of those things. So I had a little advantage that way. But not because I was smarter. I just-- I knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about after school? What did you do for enjoyment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was standard for a Jewish boy to go to public school in the morning and go to cheder in the afternoon. And there was standard procedure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you taught in that cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we studied the [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your teacher's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [? Poluka, ?] sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. It's just known as [? Poluka, ?] and everybody knew him as that-- by that name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this place that you studied?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in-- at his home, at the-- at the teacher's home in his-- I recall, his front room was set up with tables and chairs and long benches. And we sat there, and you probably see typical pictures of a cheder in-- of the-- of that period, and when I see them, I see myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you wear a yarmulke?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but not every day. I mean not throughout the day. Only when I went cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you learned in that cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were probably about 15 or 20 of us, various ages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I liked-- I like stories, so it was very interesting for me to learn [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the bar mitzvah. Where did you celebrate your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was bar mitzvah, I was in camp by that time. So my bar mitzvah was just that. My camp was my bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever celebrated a-- an occasion which is a bar mitzvah for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I'm-- I'm planning to have one.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great. That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I attended a bar mitzvah not too long ago, someone who was approximately my age, and he celebrated his 13-- his bar mitzvah exactly 55 years after his 13th birthday. So I still have a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'll do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I act as though I had a bar mitzvah anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in that when I am called to the Torah, I, you know, I recite the prayers in the same way that anybody who had bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember those prayers well from your cheder time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister told me that you like to sing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I won't to ask you to sing, but do you remember being taught songs in cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- we-- we had a-- for our city, it was kind of a progressive cheder. We didn't just learn the Torah. We learned arithmetic and other secular things. So in that way, we were kind of advanced.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the music?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we learned songs, folk songs that we used to sing. It-- it was a good idea. I guess he was a really good teacher, because he knew what children need and what motivates them, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember one in particular that you want to share?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no. It's so-- it's so traditional. You know. We learned [INAUDIBLE] on mandolin and [INAUDIBLE], which was for us a-- the place where we were, really. You know, we were doing the same-- the things that we were singing about. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a about-- a little bit more about your childhood. When you weren't in cheder and you weren't in school, what might a young boy do in Narewka for fun, for entertainment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Narewka, along the-- Narewka-- the city of Narewka, there is a river. And for us, this was a-- a place to be. We-- in the summertime, we go-- we went swimming there. In the wintertime, we skated on it. And it was just-- there was a meadow between our-- the buildings or the homes and-- and the river, and as children, I recall as a very pleasant time to have-- to be able to just run out there and go swimming. In the summertime, we mostly ran around barefoot, you know, really free souls. And as I said earlier, we had our grandparents on either side of the street, and we had friends and uncles and aunts and-- who lived almost across the street from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the non-Jewish community, the Gentile community there? What kind of interaction did you have with the people-- the other people of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, the-- during my period of time, the-- my memory, the relationship between the Jews and the non-Jews was very congenial in that there was sort of a symbiotic relationship in that there was an exchange in commerce and exchange in goods and production, for example.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about as a child? Did you play with other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had neighbors who were non-Jewish neighbors and as children we played together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember ever hearing any kind of anti-Semitic comment from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anti-Semitism in Poland, especially in eastern Poland, was a way of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it a way of life for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- I-- it was so strong and it was so common that we just accepted it, and we-- we just worked around it.

speaker: interviewer
text: For instance, what did you come across?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for example, we had this woman who lived almost across the street from us, and she used to chase around because she didn't want us to cross-- to walk in front of her house. So when we would try to walk in front of her house, she would chase us away. And so we had to go across the street to go past her house. But that's OK. We called her names for that, and that was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she-- why didn't she want you to walk in front of her house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were Jewish kids. She didn't want any Jews in front of her house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents aware of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever approach her and speak to her about It

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. Oh no. It was so-- such a common thing that if one had to-- wanted to do this with everyone who was anti-Semitic, you would be spending your life just speaking to people about it. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got used to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was something that. You know, during our period of time there weren't any atrocities committed and anti-Semitism wasn't carried very far, other than some little incidences like chasing us away from her house. And she was just a nasty woman and that's all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But before that, before my-- my time, during my parent's time as young people there were atrocities committed against Jews. There were pogroms.

speaker: interviewer
text: They told you those stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were pogroms and there were rioting.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Narewka.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Narewka, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your parents had shared those memories with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. They had to go hide and-- to escape being beaten up or worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents ever speak, perhaps, that it might be a good idea to leave Narewka because of these anti-Jewish sentiments?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so. At least I don't recall that. You know, we considered our place where we lived our home, and there was no-- not much thought of moving. However, there were no opportunities for young people in a small town like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what brought you then to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, you know, since he had the opportunity to move out of Narewka, and it was-- the young people wanted to move naturally. They looked for-- tried to look for opportunities.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father left about-- in '32 or '33?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you join your father?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at the time then?

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- I was eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about moving to a new city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was exciting. I-- I-- I was sorry to leave my grandparents, but I was excited to come to Krakow and to live with-- where my father was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go to school when you arrived in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as soon as we arrived in Krakow, we were sent to school and continued our education.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the school you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall the name of the school. I should-- I should remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a public school, and it was within walking distance from where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is it that you lived? Do you remember the street name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- we lived on [INAUDIBLE], a street called [INAUDIBLE], which means industrial city-- industrial street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that considered a good neighborhood? A--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a-- not a-- it was a good neighborhood, but it wasn't a high-class neighborhood. It was really an industrial area where my father worked, near his-- the company where he worked. And the housing was actually factory housing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have Jewish neighbors in that neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not immediately in the-- in the neighborhood. There was one at the end of the street, but not many-- not many Jewish neighbors in that area. That was not a Jewish area. We were-- were were across the river. Krakow is a city that's divided by a river, and on one side of the-- of the river, it's actually called by a different name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Podgorze. And Krakow was on the other side. And we lived in Podgorze, and the Jewish section-- the Jewish sector of Krakow was across the river in Krakow, in the other side-- on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel in any way alienated, then, being one of the only Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. We were too busy trying to-- to acclimate ourselves to-- to the new environment. And, you know, I was looking at the tall buildings, the first time I ever saw a tall building or a street car. So there was no time for any of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your family life different from when you arrived in Krakow? What changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, quite a bit in that my mother was isolated by then. You know, she-- in Narewka, she was surrounded by her family and friends. And when she moved to Krakow, she's just had to start all over again. It's like practically moving to a new country.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect her mood with the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not much. She was-- she was a strong woman. She-- she was efficient and hard-working, so that when she had-- when something had to be done, she just went to it and did it. She may-- may have fallen apart later on, you know, but when it was-- the thing had to be done, she-- she just went ahead and did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you continue living in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we continued until the war broke out. It broke-- we-- we had only been there one year and the war broke out in 1939 and Germans came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: When that started, of course, it was, as we know now, was the beginning of a very dark, dark period in-- in history of Europe and in-- in our personal history.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened, then, for you personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as soon as the Germans came in, their campaign against Jews was immediately apparent, although in the beginning, it looked like just occasional drunken soldiers bored with being-- you know, just bored with army life and just having fun with Jews, beating them up or making them do things that are--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that happen? Did you see beatings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, sure. And dragging them down the street making and making them clean the streets or kneel or whatever just to humiliate them. But as time went on, it was not long before we knew that there was a definite campaign that they're-- they were waging against the Jewish population. Because the laws were being passed almost daily.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what about for you as a child? What was affected in your life? What changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, immediately, as soon as the Germans came in, we were immediately thrown out of school. Jewish children couldn't go to public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd that feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know the immediate feeling as a boy I recall I was, well, I don't have to go to school. But then that only lasted for 10 seconds. Because there was everything that was going to go on in school was passing me by.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with your free time then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because my father's work and his life is a middle-class level of-- of income, as soon as the war broke out, his savings and his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] security disappeared immediately. So really as soon as-- as the war broke out we had to start looking around to see how we could help each other and help the family to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about going back to the Narewka? Was that a possibility?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not a possibility.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: That option was not there. When the Germans occupied Poland in 1939, the Russians occupied the eastern part of Poland. When that happened, Narewka became under the control of the Soviets, so there was no way to-- to move from one side to the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to still keep-- keep up contact with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Just occasionally somebody smuggled across the border and ended up-- came from Narewka. We had-- we used to have good contact because those people who worked in the factory where my father was the tool and die maker also came from Narewka, where they had non-Jews who had connections and family in Narewka. So they worked in Krakow and had family in Narewka.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was the Krakow Glass Factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you say that in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever visit your father there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Lots, lots of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I used to hang around his place of work and watch him do his machining. And so I picked up a little bit of that from just watching him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you imagine that that's what you might want to do when you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, uh-huh. Sure. I really did.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. This is the end of tape one, we'll continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
